summary: Gruber examines Uber's announcement of self-driving car testing in Pittsburgh, noting the company's aggressive talent acquisition from Carnegie Mellon and CEO Kalanick's blunt admission that human drivers are a stopgap to be eliminated. He challenges the assumption that Google leads in autonomous vehicles, citing his experience riding in a Mercedes prototype that traveled at highway speeds while looking like a normal sedan. Gruber argues that design matters as much as technology in self-driving cars, drawing a parallel to how the personal computer industry eventually learned the same lesson. Despite the current need for safety drivers, he sees the Pittsburgh test as a genuine milestone marking the cusp of the biggest change in automotive history.
key_points: Uber's 'strategic partnership' with Carnegie Mellon was effectively a talent raid on the university's robotics department
Kalanick's open declaration that Uber's million-plus drivers are a stopgap to be eliminated is extraordinary corporate bluntness
Google's self-driving leadership is questionable — Mercedes has highway-speed prototypes that look like normal sedans without obtrusive sensors
Design matters for self-driving cars just as it did for personal computers — ungainly sensor arrays will be a competitive disadvantage
Despite the need for safety drivers, real-world autonomous rides for regular customers represent a genuine industry milestone
money_quotes: Their 'partnership' was like that between a fox and a henhouse.
Jobs that can be performed by machines eventually will be performed by machines. That's been the steady march of progress since the dawn of the industrial revolution.
It really is extraordinary for a CEO to flatly declare that he considers the company's workforce not to be an asset, but rather a stopgap measure he's committed to eliminate.
Say what you want about the politics of his bluntness, but he certainly deserves points for honesty.
The engineers I spoke with at Mercedes were adamant that even now, while the technology is still in development and years away from production, the cars need to look beautiful.
The technology isn't everything — design matters.
Cars are status symbols — even cars you don't own. What else explains the existence of black town cars?
We're on the cusp of the biggest change to the car industry, ever.
key_insight: Self-driving car competition will ultimately be won not just on technological capability but on design — the companies that make autonomous vehicles look beautiful rather than ungainly will have a decisive advantage.
